claim.content: In measurements on single crystals of YbRh2Si2 the low-temperature electrical resistivity as a function of temperature T and magnetic field B applied perpendicular to the c axis is described for B > 0.06 T by a Landau Fermi-liquid form ρ(T,B) = ρ0(B) + A(B) T^2, where ρ0(B) is the residual resistivity and A(B) is the transport coefficient; the empirically determined A(B) exhibits a drastic, step-like reduction when B is increased from below to above B* = (9.5 ± 0.5) T, which indicates a step-like decrease of the quasiparticle effective mass in the Fermi-liquid picture; applying the empirical low-field scaling relation A ∝ γ^2, where γ(B) is the electronic specific-heat coefficient defined by Cel(T,B) = γ(B) T + … and using the same A/γ^2 proportionality constant determined at low fields, the electronic specific-heat coefficient is estimated to be γ(16 T) ≈ 70 mJ mol−1 K−2 at B = 16 T.

conclusion.content: In measurements on single crystals of YbRh2Si2 the low-temperature electrical resistivity as a function of temperature T and magnetic field B applied perpendicular to the c axis is described for B > 0.06 T by a Landau Fermi-liquid form ρ(T,B) = ρ0(B) + A(B) T^2, where ρ0(B) is the residual resistivity and A(B) is the transport coefficient; the empirically determined A(B) exhibits a drastic, step-like reduction when B is increased from below to above B* = (9.5 ± 0.5) T, which indicates a step-like decrease of the quasiparticle effective mass in the Fermi-liquid picture; applying the empirical low-field scaling relation A ∝ γ^2, where γ(B) is the electronic specific-heat coefficient defined by Cel(T,B) = γ(B) T + … and using the same A/γ^2 proportionality constant determined at low fields, the electronic specific-heat coefficient is estimated to be γ(16 T) ≈ 70 mJ mol−1 K−2 at B = 16 T.

premises.content:
Let A(B) denote the coefficient of the T^2 term in the low-temperature electrical resistivity ρ(T,B) = ρ0(B) + A(B) T^2 and let γ(B) denote the electronic specific-heat coefficient defined by Cel(T,B) = γ(B) T + …; although an empirical scaling relation A ∝ γ^2 has been demonstrated for YbRh2Si2 up to magnetic fields of approximately 4 T in previous work, we assume for the purpose of estimating γ at 16 T that this proportionality relation continues to hold with the same proportionality constant at much higher fields (for example at 16 T), so that the measured reduction of A(B) at 16 T can be converted into an estimate γ(16 T) ≈ 70 mJ mol−1 K−2 by applying the same A/γ^2 ratio determined at low fields.
      P. Gegenwart, et al., Phys. Rev. Lett. 89 (2002) 056402

For four-terminal AC resistivity measurements on single crystals of YbRh2Si2 the measured resistivity is modeled as ρ(T,B) = ρ0(B) + A(B) T^2 and least-squares fits to this T^2 form are applied over the reported low-temperature interval (for example, below a few hundred millikelvin depending on field); we assume that within the temperature range used for the fits the resistivity is well described by the pure T^2 behavior so that the fitted values of A(B) are reliable and that systematic uncertainties from higher-temperature scattering channels, finite-temperature crossovers, and sample-dependent inhomogeneities are small compared to the observed step-like change of A(B) across B*.

steps.reasoning: Treat as known from earlier reasoning that a broadened anomaly occurs at $B^{\star}\approx 9.5\,\text{T}$ with step-like features in susceptibility and magnetostriction and with a magnetization of $\sim 1\,\mu_{\mathrm{B}}$ at that field (results summarized in the established conclusion about the anomaly).

Define the electrical resistivity measurement and Fermi-liquid (FL) relation used: denote the electrical resistivity as $\rho(T,B)$, define the residual resistivity as $\rho_{0}$, and define the FL coefficient as $A(B)$ appearing in the relation $\rho(T,B)=\rho_{0}+A(B)T^{2}$ valid in a Landau Fermi liquid; the authors report that this FL $T^{2}$ behavior is observed for fields $B>0.06\,\text{T}$ applied in the easy plane ($B\perp c$).
[5]
Fig. 2

Describe the experimental determination of $A(B)$: resistivity $\rho(T,B)$ was measured using the standard four-terminal AC technique on the same high-quality single crystals, and fits of the low-temperature $\rho(T)$ data to $\rho_{0}+A(B)T^{2}$ yield the field-dependent coefficient $A(B)$; the inset of the figure shows $\rho$ vs $T$ for representative fields (8 and 16\,T) with solid lines representing the $T^{2}$ fits.
Fig. 2

State the observed field dependence of $A(B)$: $A(B)$ is drastically reduced upon increasing $B$ from below to above $B^{\star}$; in the authors' presentation $A(B)$ vs $B$ (on a logarithmic vertical axis) shows a strong, step-like decrease when crossing $B^{\star}$, indicating a rapid drop of the FL scattering coefficient for $B>B^{\star}$.
Fig. 2

Define the electronic specific-heat coefficient $\gamma$ as the linear-in-temperature coefficient of the electronic specific heat (i.e., $C_{\mathrm{el}}(T)=\gamma T+\dots$) and recall the empirically established scaling relation reported by earlier work that $A\propto\gamma^{2}$ holds at least up to $4\,\text{T}$; cite the source of that scaling.
[5]

Apply the scaling $A\propto\gamma^{2}$ to the measured reduction of $A(B)$: using the value of the proportionality $A/\gamma^{2}$ determined in the earlier work, and inserting the measured (reduced) $A(B)$ at high field (for example at $16\,\text{T}$), the electronic specific-heat coefficient $\gamma$ is estimated to be about $70\,\text{mJ}\,\text{mol}^{-1}\,\text{K}^{-2}$ at $16\,\text{T}$; this represents a much reduced $\gamma$ relative to low-field values.
Fig. 2
[5]

Conclude that the drastic, step-like reduction of $A(B)$ when increasing $B$ through $B^{\star}$ indicates a step-like decrease of the quasiparticle effective mass (via the $A\propto\gamma^{2}$ relation) and yields a quantitative estimate of the reduced $\gamma\approx 70\,\text{mJ}\,\text{mol}^{-1}\,\text{K}^{-2}$ at $16\,\text{T}$.

motivating_questions.content: Quantitative determination of the field-evolution of quasiparticle mass and the electronic density of states requires transport and thermodynamic parameters at large fields. Earlier work established $A\propto\gamma^2$ up to intermediate fields ($\sim 4\,\text{T}$), but direct measurements of $A(B)$ beyond that range were absent. Without large-field resistivity data one cannot determine whether the heavy effective mass survives to high fields or is rapidly suppressed, nor can one quantify the residual electronic specific heat $\gamma$ in the high-field regime. This lack prevented establishing whether the heavy-Fermion state persists continuously, collapses gradually, or is suppressed abruptly at a particular field scale.

paper:812369321199140865.authors: Y Tokiwa | P Gegenwart | F Weickert | R Küchler | J Custers | J Ferstl | C Geibel | F Steglich
paper:812369321199140865.en_title: Suppression of the Kondo state in YbRh2Si2 by large magnetic fields
paper:812369321199140865.publication_name: Journal of Magnetism and Magnetic Materials _ Journal of Magnetism and Magnetic Materials
paper:812369321199140865.zh_title: 强磁场对YbRh₂Si₂中近藤态的抑制作用
